item.title: ÊÝπÊçÆÁà±Ëç∑ÂçéÂ∑ûÂÖ¨Âõ≠È¢ÜÂØºËÄÖÂçè‰ºöÔºà Iowa Park Leader ÔºâÁöÑÊ≥®ÂÜåËê•ÂÖªÂ∏àÁöÑËØ¥Ê≥ïÔºåÂÅ•Ë∫´ÂΩ±ÂìçËÄÖÊØèÂ§©Êé®ËçêÁöÑËøôÁßçËõãÁôΩË¥®Ê£íÂê´ÊúâÊØîÂø´È§êÂ•∂ÈÖ™Ê±âÂÝ°Êõ¥Â§öÁöÑÈ•±ÂíåËÑÇËÇ™
item.description:
The wellness internet loves a protein bar that looks virtuous, tastes like dessert, and slides into an afternoon workout routine without a second thought. But several bars being hyped by fitness influencers hide a surprisingly dense load of saturated fat‚Äîenough to rival, and sometimes exceed, what you‚Äôd get in a drive‚Äëthru cheeseburger. As one registered dietitian told me, ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre basically eating a candy bar with a protein halo.‚Äù

The saturated fat surprise

Many influencer‚Äëapproved bars clock in at 8‚Äì11g of saturated fat, while a standard fast‚Äëfood cheeseburger lands around 6g of saturated fat. For context, the Dietary Guidelines advise keeping saturated fat under 10% of daily calories, and the American Heart Association suggests closer to 6%‚Äîabout 13 grams for a 2,000‚Äëcalorie diet. ‚ÄúOne bar can torch half your day‚Äôs budget before dinner,‚Äù said a registered dietitian, ‚Äúand that math adds up fast.‚Äù

Why influencers keep recommending it

On camera, these bars look clean, list ‚Äúwhey‚Äù or ‚Äúpea‚Äù protein, and promise satiety in a portable wrapper. The macros appear friendly‚Äî20g protein, modest carbs, and a sweet‚Äëtooth‚Äëapproved flavor. The catch is the fat matrix: ingredients like palm kernel oil, cocoa butter, or added coconut can quietly spike saturated fat, while sugar alcohols and chicory fiber blunt the perceived sugar hit.

What that means for your goals

High saturated fat isn‚Äôt just a heart‚Äëhealth footnote; it can alter the nutrient trade‚Äëoffs of your snack. If 40‚Äì60% of a bar‚Äôs calories come from fat, you‚Äôre paying a protein premium for something that behaves like a candy bar metabolically. ‚ÄúFor muscle repair, what you need is protein quality and overall diet pattern‚Äînot stealthy saturated fat,‚Äù the RD explained.

The protein quality puzzle

Some bars pair decent protein with fatty binders, driving that dessert‚Äëlike texture you see on TikTok. Others use milk‚Äëderived or soy isolates that digest well, but then compensate for flavor with layers, coatings, and oil‚Äëheavy fillers. ‚ÄúA great bar doesn‚Äôt need a fudge‚Äëstyle shell or a glossy glaze to taste good,‚Äù said the registered dietitian. ‚ÄúIf the coating melts like chocolate, you‚Äôre often getting dessert levels of saturated fat.‚Äù

How to read the label like a pro

Before you toss a bar into your gym bag, do a 10‚Äësecond scan that cuts through the marketing fog:

  • Look for ‚â§3g saturated fat per bar, ‚â•15‚Äì20g protein, and ‚â§8g added sugar; favor oils like canola or sunflower, not palm kernel or coconut oil.

When a high‚Äëfat bar can still fit

Context matters, not panic. If the rest of your day is rich in unsaturated fats‚Äîthink olive oil, nuts, and fish‚Äîan occasional high‚Äëfat bar won‚Äôt torpedo your health. Pre‚Äëhike or long travel days, a more energy‚Äëdense bar might even be useful. The goal is informed choice, not all‚Äëor‚Äënothing thinking.

Better swaps that still feel like a treat

If you crave a dessert‚Äëleaning bite, pick bars that use crisped soy, whey isolate, or nut‚Äëand‚Äëseed bases with mostly unsaturated fats. Greek yogurt with whey powder and fruit gives a creamy‚Äësweet profile without the stealthy saturated load. Cottage cheese, cacao nibs, and a drizzle of honey can hit the same notes, minus the oil‚Äëheavy coating.

The blood‚Äëwork angle you can‚Äôt see on social

Influencer abs don‚Äôt show LDL cholesterol, but your labs will. Habitual high saturated fat can raise LDL, a key risk factor for heart disease, even in active, lean people. ‚ÄúFitness doesn‚Äôt grant immunity from nutrition basics,‚Äù said the registered dietitian. ‚ÄúYour arteries don‚Äôt watch Reels‚Äîthey respond to what you actually eat.‚Äù

Red flags on the wrapper

Be wary of vague claims like ‚Äúketo,‚Äù ‚Äúzero guilt,‚Äù or ‚Äúclean energy‚Äù without a balanced macro picture. A long ingredient list featuring multiple oils, sugar alcohols, and stabilizers is a telltale sign. If fiber is sky‚Äëhigh but your stomach feels off, you may be reacting to chicory root or inulin‚Äëheavy formulas.

A simple rule of thumb

If a bar tastes like a candy bar, check whether it‚Äôs built like one too. Aim for protein‚Äëforward, fiber‚Äësupportive, and fat that‚Äôs mostly unsaturated. Think of saturated fat as a budget: spend it on foods you truly love, not on a glossy coating hiding under a wellness filter.

What to do next

Audit your go‚Äëto snack: read the label, note the saturated fat, and compare it to a basic cheeseburger for a reality check. If the number is higher, rotate in a lower‚Äësat‚Äëfat option most days and save the heavy hitter for specific situations. ‚ÄúSmall swaps beat willpower every time,‚Äù the dietitian said. ‚ÄúPick a bar that serves your goals, not just your feed.‚Äù
item.author: Iowa Park Leader

item.title: ‰∏∫‰ªÄ‰πà‰∏ÄËß¶Âç≥ÂèØÂ°ëÈÄÝÊÇ®Â§öÂπ¥ÁöÑÊÉÖÊÑüÂíå‰∫∫ÈôÖÂÖ≥Á≥ª- Earth.com
item.description:
There‚Äôs a peculiar thing about memory. Ask someone to describe their grandmother and they might struggle for words, but mention the way she used to pat their cheek and something shifts in their face.

The recollection arrives not as information but as sensation, almost as if the cheek itself is doing the remembering.

For a long time, scientists have not really known what to do with experiences like this. Touch has been filed away as a sensory matter, useful for telling silk from sandpaper or hot from cold.

But a recent paper argues that we have been looking at touch with the wrong end of the telescope.

The most important thing about it, the authors suggest, is not what it tells us about the world but what it does to our inner lives ‚Äì sometimes for the rest of those lives.

Touch shapes emotional memory

The new work brings together decades of scattered findings from neuroscience, psychology, physiology, and clinical research into a single, coherent picture.

The researchers introduce a concept called affective tactile memory. It may sound complex, but it describes something very familiar.

This concept suggests that meaningful touch stays with us longer than ordinary sensations, even though science has not fully explained it until now.

Not all touch is equal

To understand this, it helps to know that your skin has different types of nerve fibers, and some are meant for gentle, caring touch.

These fibers respond best to slow and warm contact, like when a parent softly strokes a child or a partner touches you gently. They do not react much to quick, everyday actions like brushing crumbs off a table.

This shows that the body separates touch into two types. One type helps you deal with the physical world, while the other connects directly to your emotions.

Memory that hides in the body

Here is where the paper takes its most interesting turn. The authors propose that when we remember a meaningful touch, we are not simply replaying a recording filed away in the brain.

Something more strange and more intimate happens. The body itself may participate in the recollection, partially re-enacting how the original touch felt.

‚ÄúIt may be that when we remember a meaningful touch, the brain reactivates traces of how that experience felt in the body,‚Äù said Dr. Laura Crucianelli of Queen Mary University of London.

This is a different kind of memory than what we typically study. Visual memories can be examined like photographs. Verbal memories can be transcribed.

A touch memory, by this account, is something closer to a partial physical replay, faint but real, distributed across the same systems that handled the original sensation.

Touch builds trust and safety

What does any of this have to do with how a person turns out? Quite a lot, according to the authors.

‚ÄúA comforting touch doesn‚Äôt just fade; it may become part of us,‚Äù said Dr. Crucianelli.

‚ÄúThrough an interplay between sensory signals and emotional brain networks, touch experiences can be remembered both consciously and unconsciously, shaping how safe we feel, how we bond with others, and how we navigate relationships across the lifespan.‚Äù

This remarkable claim suggests that the felt experience of safety, the basic substrate for trust and attachment, is built partly from accumulated tactile memories operating mostly beneath awareness.

Long before a child can articulate that they are loved, they are being told so in a language their nervous system understands perfectly well.

Emotional touch has been overlooked

For decades, memory science has concentrated on the visual and the verbal. Pictures and words are tidy. They can be presented on a screen, recalled, and scored.

Touch is harder to study. You cannot easily run a controlled experiment on something like a hug. The result is that touch, particularly its emotional dimension, has been a blind spot.

Experts have known it matters, but the underlying mechanisms have remained scattered across disciplines that rarely speak to each other.

‚ÄúAffective touch has been largely overlooked in memory research. We show that it deserves a central place in how we understand the emotional brain,‚Äù said Dr. Crucianelli.

The paper‚Äôs contribution is to draw the map, connecting the dots between specialized nerve fibers in the skin, brain regions involved in emotion and reward, and the systems that store and retrieve experience.

Implications of the study

The model has practical reach. Conditions ranging from anxiety disorders to attachment difficulties to certain kinds of trauma involve disruptions in how touch is processed and how emotional memories are formed and retrieved.

A clearer theoretical framework gives clinicians and researchers something concrete to work with.

There are also broader cultural threads worth pulling. The authors note something that feels especially relevant now.

‚ÄúEven the most subtle forms of touch can leave lasting imprints on how we think, feel, and relate to others,‚Äù wrote Dr. Crucianelli and colleagues.

‚ÄúThis work highlights how deeply our relationships are rooted in physical, embodied experience.‚Äù

What we may be missing

Today, many of our relationships happen through screens, and this shift has made communication easier and more constant.

There is nothing wrong with this change on its own, and it helps people stay connected across distance.

Yet, an important part of human connection still depends on physical touch. When that is missing, it is not a complete substitute, even if messages and video calls feel warm and caring.

Without touch, a small but important part of connection may remain unfulfilled, something that digital interaction alone cannot fully replace.

The study is published in the journal Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews.
